easy.companyDescription: An internet retailer expanding quickly across consumer categories.
easy.macroContext: Internet valuations are peaking as investors start demanding proof of durable profits.
easy.situation: Will scale turn heavy spending into a lasting advantage, or will cash burn collide with tighter capital?
easy.longCase: Revenue is growing rapidly as more shoppers move online, and category expansion could lock in a durable position.
easy.shortCase: Losses are mounting while the stock has already run far ahead of current earnings, making a sentiment reversal costly.
setupHints: Fulfillment, marketing, and expansion spending are rising faster than near-term profit.

medium.companyDescription: A consumer-facing company investing ahead of its current earnings.
medium.macroContext: Investors are reassessing how much time unprofitable growth businesses should receive.
medium.situation: Can current spending build enough future revenue to justify a valuation built on expansion?
medium.longCase: A broader customer base could make earlier investment more productive as revenue grows.
medium.shortCase: A slower path to positive cash flow could force investors to value the business on nearer-term results.

hard.companyDescription: A public company pursuing growth before steady earnings.
hard.macroContext: Investors are placing more weight on cash generation when comparing growth businesses.
hard.situation: Does the business merit patience while its spending runs ahead of reported results?
hard.longCase: Additional customers could improve the return on earlier investment over time.
hard.shortCase: If expenses remain high, the market may favor companies with clearer current earnings.

reveal.shortText: That was Amazon. The dot-com crash wiped out most of its market value, but it survived to become one of the most valuable companies in the world.
reveal.funFact: Amazon stock fell from about $107 to $7 a share during the bust â€” a roughly 93% decline.
whyItMoved: Internet valuations collapsed as capital tightened.
Investors demanded a path to profit instead of pure growth.
The stock had already priced aggressive expansion success.

sources.label: Prototype sample seed
sources.notes: Placeholder sample content for Phase 3 pipeline; not production research.

review.reviewNotes: H029 rewrote Medium and Hard after H028 identified the late-1990s consumer-growth, heavy-reinvestment, capital-selectivity, and valuation conjunction as an Amazon triangulation path. Medium peer set: Amazon/eBay/Priceline/AOL. Hard peer set: Yahoo/eBay/Priceline/AOL/Amazon/Cisco; the prose centers the generic current-earnings tradeoff, not the answer. Easy and shared labels remain unchanged; Medium/Hard Gate 2 results were removed as stale.
